subject: Physics
question_text: Suppose that the lower half of a concave mirror's reflecting surface in given figure is covered with an opaque non-reflecting material. What effect will this have on the image of an object placed in front of the mirror?
question_html: Suppose that the lower half of a concave mirror's reflecting surface in given figure is covered with an opaque non-reflecting material. What effect will this have on the image of an object placed in front of the mirror?
solution_text: As laws of reflection are true for all points of the mirror, the image will be of the whole object (and not of half object as expected erroneously). However, as an area of the reflecting surface has been reduced, the intensity of the image will reduce.

question_text: An object is placed at (a) 10 cm (b) 5 cm in front of a concave mirror of radius of curvature 15 cm. Find the position, nature, and magnification of the image in each case.
question_html: An object is placed at (a) 10 cm (b) 5 cm in front of a concave mirror of radius of curvature 15 cm. Find the position, nature, and magnification of the image in each case.
solution_text: The focal length f {tex}= - \frac {15}{2}cm{/tex} = -7.5 cm The object distance u = -10 cm. Using the mirror relation {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} gives {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{-10}=\frac{1}{-7.5}{/tex} or {tex}v=\frac{10 \times 7.5}{-2.5}=-30 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} The image is 30 cm from the mirror on the same side as the object. Also, magnification {tex}m=-\frac{v}{u}=-\frac{(-30)}{(-10)}=-3{/tex} The image is magnified, real and inverted. The object distance u = -5 cm. Using the mirror Eq. {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{-10}=\frac{1}{-7.5}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{-5}=\frac{1}{-7.5}{/tex} or {tex}v=\frac{5 \times 7.5}{(7.5-5)}=15 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} This image is formed at 15 cm behind the mirror. It is a virtual image. Magnification {tex}m=-\frac{v}{u}=-\frac{15}{(-5)}=3{/tex} The image is magnified, virtual and erect.
solution_html:
The focal length f {tex}= - \frac {15}{2}cm{/tex} = -7.5 cm

  1. The object distance u = -10 cm. Using the mirror relation  {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} gives
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{-10}=\frac{1}{-7.5}{/tex}
    or {tex}v=\frac{10 \times 7.5}{-2.5}=-30 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
    The image is 30 cm from the mirror on the same side as the object.
    Also, magnification {tex}m=-\frac{v}{u}=-\frac{(-30)}{(-10)}=-3{/tex}
    The image is magnified, real and inverted.
  2. The object distance u = -5 cm. Using the mirror  Eq. {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{-10}=\frac{1}{-7.5}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{-5}=\frac{1}{-7.5}{/tex}
    or {tex}v=\frac{5 \times 7.5}{(7.5-5)}=15 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
    This image is formed at 15 cm behind the mirror. It is a virtual image.
    Magnification {tex}m=-\frac{v}{u}=-\frac{15}{(-5)}=3{/tex}
    The image is magnified, virtual and erect.

subject: Physics
question_text: Suppose while sitting in a parked car, you notice a jogger approaching towards you in the side view mirror of R = 2 m. If the jogger is running at a speed of 5 ms -1 , how fast the image of the jogger appear to move when the jogger is 39 m 29 m 19 m and 9 m away
question_html:
Suppose while sitting in a parked car, you notice a jogger approaching towards you in the side view mirror of R = 2 m. If the jogger is running at a speed of 5 ms-1, how fast the image of the jogger appear to move when the jogger is

  1. 39 m
  2. 29 m
  3. 19 m and
  4. 9 m away
solution_text: From the mirror equation Eq. {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} , we get {tex}v=\frac{f u}{u-f}{/tex} For convex mirror, since R = 2 m, f = 1 m. Then when jogger at u = -39 m, {tex}v=\frac{(-39) \times 1}{-39-1}=\frac{39}{40} \mathrm{m}{/tex} Since the jogger moves at a constant speed of 5 m s -1 , after 1s the position of the image v (for u = –39 + 5 = –34) is (34/35 )m. The shift in the position of the image in 1 s is (average speed of jogger 's image) given by:- {tex}\frac{39}{40}-\frac{34}{35}=\frac{1365-1360}{1400}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{1400}=\frac{1}{280} \mathrm{m}{/tex} Therefore, the average speed of the image when the jogger is between 39 m and 34 m from the mirror, is (1/280) m s -1 Similarly, it can be seen that for u = -29 m, -19 m and -9 m, the speed with which the image appears to move is {tex}\frac{1}{150} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1} ,\frac{1}{60} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{10} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} , respectively. Although the jogger has been moving with a constant speed, the speed of his/her image appears to increase substantially as he/she moves closer to the mirror. This phenomenon can be noticed by any person sitting in a stationary car or a bus. In case of moving vehicles, a similar phenomenon could be observed if the vehicle in the rear is moving closer with a constant speed.
solution_html:
From the mirror equation Eq. {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}, we get
{tex}v=\frac{f u}{u-f}{/tex}
For convex mirror, since R = 2 m, f = 1 m. Then
when jogger at u = -39 m, {tex}v=\frac{(-39) \times 1}{-39-1}=\frac{39}{40} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
Since the jogger moves at a constant speed of 5 m s-1, after 1s the position of the image v (for u = –39 + 5 = –34) is (34/35 )m.
The shift in the position of the image in 1 s is (average speed of jogger 's image) given by:-
{tex}\frac{39}{40}-\frac{34}{35}=\frac{1365-1360}{1400}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{1400}=\frac{1}{280} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
Therefore, the average speed of the image when the jogger is between 39 m and 34 m from the mirror, is (1/280) m s-1
Similarly, it can be seen that for u = -29 m, -19 m and -9 m, the speed with which the image appears to move is
{tex}\frac{1}{150} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1} ,\frac{1}{60} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{10} \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}, respectively.
Although the jogger has been moving with a constant speed, the speed of his/her image appears to increase substantially as he/she moves closer to the mirror. This phenomenon can be noticed by any person sitting in a stationary car or a bus. In case of moving vehicles, a similar phenomenon could be observed if the vehicle in the rear is moving closer with a constant speed.

question_text: A magician during a show makes a glass lens with n = 1.47 disappear in a trough of liquid. What is the refractive index of the liquid? Could the liquid be water?
question_html: A magician during a show makes a glass lens with n = 1.47 disappear in a trough of liquid. What is the refractive index of the liquid? Could the liquid be water?
solution_text: The refractive index of the liquid must be equal to 1.47 in order to make the lens disappear. This means n 1 = n 2 . This gives {tex}\frac 1f{/tex} = 0 or {tex}f \rightarrow \infty{/tex} . The lens in the liquid will act as a plain sheet of glass No, the liquid is not water. It could be glycerine.
solution_html: The refractive index of the liquid must be equal to 1.47 in order to make the lens disappear. This means n1 = n2. This gives {tex}\frac 1f{/tex} = 0 or {tex}f \rightarrow \infty{/tex}. The lens in the liquid will act as a plain sheet of glass No, the liquid is not water. It could be glycerine.

question_text: If f = 0.5 m for a glass lens, what is the power of the lens? The radii of curvature of the faces of a double convex lens are 10 cm and 15 cm. Its focal length is 12 cm. What is the refractive index of glass? A convex lens has 20 cm focal length in air. What is focal length in water? (Refractive index of air-water = 1.33, refractive index for air-glass = 1.5.)
question_html:
1. If f = 0.5 m for a glass lens, what is the power of the lens?
  2. The radii of curvature of the faces of a double convex lens are 10 cm and 15 cm. Its focal length is 12 cm. What is the refractive index of glass?
  3. A convex lens has 20 cm focal length in air. What is focal length in water? (Refractive index of air-water = 1.33, refractive index for air-glass = 1.5.)
solution_text: Power = +2 dioptre Here, we have f = +12 cm, R 1 = +10 cm, R 2 = -15 cm by using lens formula Substituting the values, we have {tex}\frac{1}{12}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{-15}\right){/tex} This gives {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.5. For a glass lens in air, {tex}\mu_2{/tex} = 1.5, {tex}\mu_1{/tex} = 1, f = +20 cm. Hence, the lens formula gives {tex}\frac{1}{20}=0.5\left[\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}{1\over R_1}-{1\over R_2} ={1\over 10}{/tex} .........(1) For the same glass lens in water, {tex}\mu_2{/tex} = 1.5, {tex}\mu_1{/tex} = 1.33. Therefore, {tex}\frac{1.33}{f}=(1.5-1.33)\left[\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right]{/tex} thus from (1) we get, {tex}{1\over f} = {{0.17\times 1}\over {1.33\times 10}}{/tex} Combining these two equations, we find f = + 78.2 cm
solution_html:
1. Power = +2 dioptre
  2. Here, we have f = +12 cm, R1 = +10 cm, R2 = -15 cm
     by using lens formula Substituting the values, we have
    {tex}\frac{1}{12}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{-15}\right){/tex}
    This gives {tex}\mu{/tex}= 1.5.
  3. For a glass lens in air, {tex}\mu_2{/tex}= 1.5, {tex}\mu_1{/tex} = 1, f = +20 cm. Hence, the lens formula gives{tex}\frac{1}{20}=0.5\left[\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right]{/tex}

    {tex}{1\over R_1}-{1\over R_2} ={1\over 10}{/tex}.........(1)
    For the same glass lens in water, {tex}\mu_2{/tex} = 1.5, {tex}\mu_1{/tex} = 1.33. Therefore,
    {tex}\frac{1.33}{f}=(1.5-1.33)\left[\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right]{/tex} thus from (1) we get,

    {tex}{1\over f} = {{0.17\times 1}\over {1.33\times 10}}{/tex}

    Combining these two equations, we find f = + 78.2 cm

subject: Physics
question_text: A small candle, 2.5 cm in size is placed at 27 cm in front of a concave mirror of radius of curvature 36 cm. At what distance from the mirror should a screen be placed in order to obtain a sharp image? Describe the nature and size of the image. If the candle is moved closer to the mirror, how would the screen have to be moved?
question_html: A small candle, 2.5 cm in size is placed at 27 cm in front of a concave mirror of radius of curvature 36 cm. At what distance from the mirror should a screen be placed in order to obtain a sharp image? Describe the nature and size of the image. If the candle is moved closer to the mirror, how would the screen have to be moved?
solution_text: The object is kept between f and C. So the image should be real, inverted and beyond C. To locate the sharp image, the screen should be placed at the position of the image. Radius of curvature of the concave mirror, R = -36 cm The focal length {tex}f=\frac{-R}{2}=-18 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} Object distance u = -27 cm Using mirror formula, {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{-27}=\frac{1}{-18}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{18}+\frac{1}{27}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{-3+2}{54} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{54}{/tex} v = -54 cm Size of image can be calculated magnification {tex}m=-\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-I}{O}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{-I}{O}=-\frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I}{+2.5}=-\frac{-54}{-27} \Rightarrow I=-5 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} So, the image is inverted and magnified. Thus in order to locate the sharp image, the screen should be kept 54 cm in front of the concave mirror, and the image on the screen will be observed real, inverted, and magnified. If the candle is moved closer to the mirror, the real image will move away from the mirror hence the screen has to be shifted away from the mirror to locate the sharp image.
solution_html:
The object is kept between f and C. So the image should be real, inverted and beyond C. To locate the sharp image, the screen should be placed at the position of the image.

Radius of curvature of the concave mirror, R = -36 cm
The focal length {tex}f=\frac{-R}{2}=-18 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
Object distance u = -27 cm
Using mirror formula, {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{-27}=\frac{1}{-18}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{18}+\frac{1}{27}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{-3+2}{54} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{54}{/tex}
v = -54 cm
Size of image can be calculated magnification
{tex}m=-\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-I}{O}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{-I}{O}=-\frac{v}{u}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{I}{+2.5}=-\frac{-54}{-27} \Rightarrow I=-5 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
So, the image is inverted and magnified. Thus in order to locate the sharp image, the screen should be kept 54 cm in front of the concave mirror, and the image on the screen will be observed real, inverted, and magnified. If the candle is moved closer to the mirror, the real image will move away from the mirror hence the screen has to be shifted away from the mirror to locate the sharp image.

question_text: A 4.5 cm needle is placed 12 cm away from a convex mirror of focal length 15 cm. Give the location of the image and the magnification. Describe what happens as the needle is moved farther from the mirror.
question_html: A 4.5 cm needle is placed 12 cm away from a convex mirror of focal length 15 cm. Give the location of the image and the magnification. Describe what happens as the needle is moved farther from the mirror.
solution_text: u = -12 cm, f = +15 cm, O = 4.5 cm As, {tex}\frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{{\text{f}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{\operatorname{f} } - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{{15}} + \frac{1}{{12}} = \frac{{4 + 5}}{{60}} = \frac{9}{{60}}{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{{60}}{9} = 6.7cm{/tex} i.e., image is formed 6.7 cm behind the convex mirror. It must be virtual and erect. As, {tex}m = \frac{I}{O} = - \frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{I}{{4.5}} = \frac{{6.7}}{{ - 12}}{/tex} or {tex}I = \frac{{6.7 \times 4.5}}{{12}}{/tex} = 2.5 cm {tex}\therefore{/tex} Image is erect, and virtual. As the needle is moved farther from the mirror, image moves away from the the mirror till it is at focus F of the mirror. The size of the image goes on decreasing.
solution_html:
u = -12 cm, f = +15 cm, O = 4.5 cm
As, {tex}\frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{{\text{f}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{\operatorname{f} } - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{{15}} + \frac{1}{{12}} = \frac{{4 + 5}}{{60}} = \frac{9}{{60}}{/tex}
{tex}v = \frac{{60}}{9} = 6.7cm{/tex}
i.e., image is formed 6.7 cm behind the convex mirror. It must be virtual and erect.
As, {tex}m = \frac{I}{O} = - \frac{v}{u}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{I}{{4.5}} = \frac{{6.7}}{{ - 12}}{/tex} or {tex}I = \frac{{6.7 \times 4.5}}{{12}}{/tex}= 2.5 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Image is erect, and virtual.
As the needle is moved farther from the mirror, image moves away from the the mirror till it is at focus F of the mirror. The size of the image goes on decreasing.

subject: Physics
question_text: A tank is filled with water to a height of 12.5 cm. The apparent depth of a needle lying at the bottom of the tank is measured by a microscope to be 9.4 cm. What is the refractive index of water? If water is replaced by a liquid of refractive index 1.63 up to the same height, by what distance would the microscope have to be moved to focus on the needle agains?
question_html: A tank is filled with water to a height of 12.5 cm. The apparent depth of a needle lying at the bottom of the tank is measured by a microscope to be 9.4 cm. What is the refractive index of water? If water is replaced by a liquid of refractive index 1.63 up to the same height, by what distance would the microscope have to be moved to focus on the needle agains?
solution_text: Case I: Given, real depth = 12.5 cm, apparent depth = 9.4 cm As, {tex}\mu = \frac{{real\;depth}}{{apparent\;depth}}{/tex} or {tex}\mu = \frac{{12.5}}{{9.4}} = 1.33{/tex} Case II : {tex}\mu = 1.63{/tex} , real depth = 12.5 cm Apparent depth {tex} = \frac{{\operatorname{Real}depth}}{\mu }{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{12.5}}{{1.63}} = 7.67cm{/tex} Distance through which microscope has to be moved up {tex} = (9.4 - 7.67)cm = 1.73cm{/tex}
solution_html:
Case I: Given, real depth = 12.5 cm, apparent depth = 9.4 cm
As, {tex}\mu = \frac{{real\;depth}}{{apparent\;depth}}{/tex}
or {tex}\mu = \frac{{12.5}}{{9.4}} = 1.33{/tex}
Case II : {tex}\mu = 1.63{/tex}, real depth = 12.5 cm
Apparent depth {tex} = \frac{{\operatorname{Real}depth}}{\mu }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{12.5}}{{1.63}} = 7.67cm{/tex}
Distance through which microscope has to be moved up
{tex} = (9.4 - 7.67)cm = 1.73cm{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: Refraction of a ray in air incident at 60° with the normal to a glass air and water air interface, respectively. Predict the angle of refraction in glass when the angle of incidence in water is 45° with the normal to a water glass interface.
question_html: Refraction of a ray in air incident at 60° with the normal to a glass air and water air interface, respectively. Predict the angle of refraction in glass when the angle of incidence in water is 45° with the normal to a water glass interface.
solution_text: For the glass-air interface: Angle of incidence, i = 60° Angle of refraction, r = 35° According to Snell’s law, {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\sin \mathrm{i}}{\sin \mathrm{r}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin 35^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{0.8660}{0.5736}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} = 1.51 For the water-air interface: Angle of incidence, i = 60° Angle of refraction, r = 47° According to Snell’s law, {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{w}}^{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{w}}^{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin 47^{\circ}}=\frac{0.87}{0.73}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{w}}^{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} = 1.19 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{w}}^{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{a}}}{\mu_{\mathrm{W}}^{2}}=\frac{1.51}{1.19}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{w}}^{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} = 1.26 For the glass- water interface: Angle of incidence, i = 45 o Angle of refraction, r = ? o According to Snell’s law, {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{w}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin 45^{\circ}}{\sin \mathrm{r}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin r = {tex}\frac{\sin \mathrm{i}}{\mu \mathrm{g}}=\frac{1 / \sqrt{2}}{1.27}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin r = 0.6 {tex}\therefore{/tex} r = sin -1 (0.6) = 36.87 o {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r {tex} \approx {/tex} 37 o Therefore, the angle of refraction in glass is 37 o
solution_html:
1. For the glass-air interface:

    Angle of incidence, i = 60°
    Angle of refraction, r = 35°
    According to Snell’s law,
    {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\sin \mathrm{i}}{\sin \mathrm{r}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin 35^{\circ}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{0.8660}{0.5736}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} = 1.51
  2. For the water-air interface:

    Angle of incidence, i = 60°
    Angle of refraction, r = 47°
    According to Snell’s law,
    {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{w}}^{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{w}}^{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin 47^{\circ}}=\frac{0.87}{0.73}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{w}}^{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} = 1.19
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{w}}^{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{a}}}{\mu_{\mathrm{W}}^{2}}=\frac{1.51}{1.19}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{w}}^{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} = 1.26
  3. For the glass- water interface:

    Angle of incidence, i = 45o
    Angle of refraction, r = ?o
    According to Snell’s law,
    {tex}\mu_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{w}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin 45^{\circ}}{\sin \mathrm{r}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin r = {tex}\frac{\sin \mathrm{i}}{\mu \mathrm{g}}=\frac{1 / \sqrt{2}}{1.27}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin r = 0.6
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} r = sin-1 (0.6) = 36.87o
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r {tex} \approx {/tex} 37o
    Therefore, the angle of refraction in glass is 37o

question_text: A small bulb is placed at the bottom of a tank containing water to a depth of 80 cm. What is the area of the surface of water through which light from the bulb can emerge out? Refractive index of water is 1.33. (Consider the bulb to be a point source.)
question_html: A small bulb is placed at the bottom of a tank containing water to a depth of 80 cm. What is the area of the surface of water through which light from the bulb can emerge out? Refractive index of water is 1.33. (Consider the bulb to be a point source.)
solution_text: In right angled triangle ABC, {tex}\tan \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)=\frac{h}{r} \Rightarrow \cot \theta=\frac{h}{r}{/tex} .... (i) where h is the depth of water and r is the radius of a water surface. Given: {tex}\mu=\frac{4}{3}{/tex} , h = 80 cm = 0.8 m Now, {tex}\mu=\frac{1}{\sin \theta}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \sin \theta=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} {tex}\cot \theta=\frac{\sqrt{7}}{3}{/tex} From eqn. (i), {tex}\frac{\sqrt{7}}{3}=\frac{0.8}{r}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 0.9 m Surface area (A) = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex} {tex}=3.14 \times 0.9^{2}{/tex} = 2.5 m 2
solution_html:
In right angled triangle ABC,
{tex}\tan \left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)=\frac{h}{r} \Rightarrow \cot \theta=\frac{h}{r}{/tex} .... (i)
where h is the depth of water and r is the radius of a water surface.
Given: {tex}\mu=\frac{4}{3}{/tex}, h = 80 cm = 0.8 m
Now, {tex}\mu=\frac{1}{\sin \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \sin \theta=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\cot \theta=\frac{\sqrt{7}}{3}{/tex}
From eqn. (i),
{tex}\frac{\sqrt{7}}{3}=\frac{0.8}{r}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 0.9 m
Surface area (A) = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=3.14 \times 0.9^{2}{/tex}
= 2.5 m2

question_text: A prism is made of glass of unknown refractive index. A parallel beam of light is incident on a face of the prism. The angle of minimum deviation is measured to be 40°. What is the refractive index of the material of the prism? The refracting angle of the prism is 60°. If the prism is placed in water (refractive index 1.33) predict the new angle of minimum deviation of a parallel beam of light.
question_html: A prism is made of glass of unknown refractive index. A parallel beam of light is incident on a face of the prism. The angle of minimum deviation is measured to be 40°. What is the refractive index of the material of the prism? The refracting angle of the prism is 60°. If the prism is placed in water (refractive index 1.33) predict the new angle of minimum deviation of a parallel beam of light.
solution_text: Given: A = 60°, {tex}\delta_m = 40^\circ {/tex} Now, {tex}_a{\mu _g} = \frac{{\sin \left( {\frac{{A + \delta m}}{2}} \right)}}{{\sin \frac{A}{2}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sin \left(60^{\circ}+40^{\circ}\right) / 2}{\sin 60^{\circ} / 2}{/tex} {tex}_a{\mu _g} = \frac{{\sin 50^\circ }}{{\sin 30^\circ }} = \frac{{0.766}}{{0.54}} = 1.532{/tex} After the prism is placed in water, {tex}_w{\mu _g} = \frac{\frac{{\sin \left( {A + \delta {'_m}} \right)}}{2}}{sin\frac{A}{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{^a{\mu _g}}}{{^\alpha {\mu _w}}} = \frac{{\sin \left( {\frac{{60 + \delta {'_m}}}{2}} \right)}}{{\sin 30^\circ }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \sin \left( {30' + \frac{{\delta {'_m}}}{2}} \right) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{1.532}}{{1.33}} {/tex} = 0.5759 or {tex}30^\circ + \frac{{\delta {'_m}}}{2} = 35^\circ 10'{/tex} or {tex}\delta {'_m} = 10^\circ 20'{/tex} Thus, the new angle of minimum deviation of a parallel beam of light is 10 o 20'.
solution_html:
Given: A = 60°, {tex}\delta_m = 40^\circ {/tex}
Now, {tex}_a{\mu _g} = \frac{{\sin \left( {\frac{{A + \delta m}}{2}} \right)}}{{\sin \frac{A}{2}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sin \left(60^{\circ}+40^{\circ}\right) / 2}{\sin 60^{\circ} / 2}{/tex}
{tex}_a{\mu _g} = \frac{{\sin 50^\circ }}{{\sin 30^\circ }} = \frac{{0.766}}{{0.54}} = 1.532{/tex}
After the prism is placed in water,
{tex}_w{\mu _g} = \frac{\frac{{\sin \left( {A + \delta {'_m}} \right)}}{2}}{sin\frac{A}{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{{^a{\mu _g}}}{{^\alpha {\mu _w}}} = \frac{{\sin \left( {\frac{{60 + \delta {'_m}}}{2}} \right)}}{{\sin 30^\circ }}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \sin \left( {30' + \frac{{\delta {'_m}}}{2}} \right) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{1.532}}{{1.33}} {/tex} = 0.5759
or {tex}30^\circ + \frac{{\delta {'_m}}}{2} = 35^\circ 10'{/tex}
or {tex}\delta {'_m} = 10^\circ 20'{/tex}
Thus, the new angle of minimum deviation of a parallel beam of light is 10o20'.

question_text: Double-convex lenses are to be manufactured from a glass of refractive index 1.55, with both faces of the same radius of curvature. What is the radius of curvature required if the focal length is to be 20cm?
question_html: Double-convex lenses are to be manufactured from a glass of refractive index 1.55, with both faces of the same radius of curvature. What is the radius of curvature required if the focal length is to be 20cm?
solution_text: Given: {tex}\mu = 1.55{/tex} , f = 20 cm The radius of curvature of one face of the lens = R 1 Radius of curvature of the other face of the lens = R 2 Radius of curvature of the double-convex lens = R {tex}\therefore R _ { 1 } = R \text { and } R _ { 2 } = - R{/tex} The value of R can be calculated using lens-maker formula as: {tex}\frac { 1 } { f } = ( \mu - 1 ) \left[ \frac { 1 } { R _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { R _ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac { 1 } { 20 } = ( 1.55 - 1 ) \left[ \frac { 1 } { R } + \frac { 1 } { R } \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac { 1 } { 20 } = 0.55 \times \frac { 2 } { R }{/tex} {tex}\therefore R = 0.55 \times 2 \times 20 = 22 \mathrm { cm }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\mu = 1.55{/tex}, f = 20 cm
The radius of curvature of one face of the lens = R1
Radius of curvature of the other face of the lens = R2
Radius of curvature of the double-convex lens = R
{tex}\therefore R _ { 1 } = R \text { and } R _ { 2 } = - R{/tex}
The value of R can be calculated using lens-maker formula as:
{tex}\frac { 1 } { f } = ( \mu - 1 ) \left[ \frac { 1 } { R _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { R _ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 20 } = ( 1.55 - 1 ) \left[ \frac { 1 } { R } + \frac { 1 } { R } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 20 } = 0.55 \times \frac { 2 } { R }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore R = 0.55 \times 2 \times 20 = 22 \mathrm { cm }{/tex}

question_text: A beam of light converges at a point P. Now a lens is placed in the path of the convergent beam 12 cm from P. At what point does the beam converge if the lens is a convex lens of a focal length 20 cm and a concave lens of focal length 16 cm?
question_html:
A beam of light converges at a point P. Now a lens is placed in the path of the convergent beam 12 cm from P. At what point does the beam converge if the lens is

  1. a convex lens of a focal length 20 cm and
  2. a concave lens of focal length 16 cm?
solution_text: Here, the point P on the right of the lens acts as a virtual object. f = 20 cm, u = 12 cm Since {tex}\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{{20}} + \frac{1}{{12}} = \frac{{3 + 5}}{{60}} = \frac{8}{{60}}{/tex} or {tex}v = \frac{{60}}{8} = 7.5cm{/tex} Image is at 7.5 cm to the right of the lens, where the beam converges. f = -16cm, u = 12 cm {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{u} = - \frac{1}{{16}} + \frac{1}{{12}} = \frac{{ - 3 + 4}}{{48}} = \frac{1}{{48}}{/tex} or v = 48 cm Hence the image is at 48 cm to the right of the lens, where the beam would converge.
solution_html:
Here, the point P on the right of the lens acts as a virtual object.

  1. f = 20 cm, u = 12 cm
    Since {tex}\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{{20}} + \frac{1}{{12}} = \frac{{3 + 5}}{{60}} = \frac{8}{{60}}{/tex}
    or {tex}v = \frac{{60}}{8} = 7.5cm{/tex}
    Image is at 7.5 cm to the right of the lens, where the beam converges.
  2. f = -16cm, u = 12 cm
    {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} + \frac{1}{u} = - \frac{1}{{16}} + \frac{1}{{12}} = \frac{{ - 3 + 4}}{{48}} = \frac{1}{{48}}{/tex}
    or v = 48 cm
    Hence the image is at 48 cm to the right of the lens, where the beam would converge.

question_text: An object of size 3.0 cm is placed 14 cm in front of a concave lens of focal length 21 cm. Describe the image produced by the lens. What happens if the object is moved further away from the lens?
question_html: An object of size 3.0 cm is placed 14 cm in front of a concave lens of focal length 21 cm. Describe the image produced by the lens. What happens if the object is moved further away from the lens?
solution_text: O = 3.0 cm, u = -14cm, f = -21cm Now, {tex}\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{\operatorname{f} } + \frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{1}{{21}} - \frac{1}{{14}}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{{35}}{{14 \times 21}}{/tex} or v = -8.4 cm The image is located 8.4 cm, from the lens on the same side as the object. As, {tex}m = \frac{I}{O} = \frac{v}{u}{/tex} {tex}I = \frac{v}{u} \times O = \frac{{ - 8.4}}{{ - 14}} \times 3{/tex} = 1.8 cm As size of image is +ve. So, image is erect and virtual of smaller size. As the object is moved away from the lens, the virtual image moves towards the focus of the lens but never beyond. The image progressively diminishes in size.
solution_html:
O = 3.0 cm, u = -14cm, f = -21cm
Now, {tex}\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{\operatorname{f} } + \frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{1}{{21}} - \frac{1}{{14}}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{{35}}{{14 \times 21}}{/tex} or v = -8.4 cm
The image is located 8.4 cm, from the lens on the same side as the object.
As, {tex}m = \frac{I}{O} = \frac{v}{u}{/tex}
{tex}I = \frac{v}{u} \times O = \frac{{ - 8.4}}{{ - 14}} \times 3{/tex} = 1.8 cm
As size of image is +ve. So, image is erect and virtual of smaller size.
As the object is moved away from the lens, the virtual image moves towards the focus of the lens but never beyond. The image progressively diminishes in size.

question_text: What is the focal length of a convex lens of focal length 30 cm in contact with a concave lens of focal length 20 cm? Is the system a converging or a diverging lens? Ignore thickness of the lenses.
question_html: What is the focal length of a convex lens of focal length 30 cm in contact with a concave lens of focal length 20 cm? Is the system a converging or a diverging lens? Ignore thickness of the lenses.
solution_text: Focal length of the convex lens, f 1 = 30cm Focal length of the concave lens, f 2 = -20cm Focal length of the system of lenses = f Since, {tex}\frac{1}{{\text{f}}} = \frac{1}{{{{\text{f}}_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{{\text{f}}_2}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{{\text{f}}} = \frac{1}{{30}} - \frac{1}{{20}} = \frac{{2 - 3}}{{60}} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{60}}{/tex} f = -60 cm The combination of lenses behaves as a concave lens. The system is not converging.
solution_html:
Focal length of the convex lens, f1 = 30cm
Focal length of the concave lens, f2 = -20cm
Focal length of the system of lenses = f
Since, {tex}\frac{1}{{\text{f}}} = \frac{1}{{{{\text{f}}_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{{\text{f}}_2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{{\text{f}}} = \frac{1}{{30}} - \frac{1}{{20}} = \frac{{2 - 3}}{{60}} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{60}}{/tex}
f = -60 cm
The combination of lenses behaves as a concave lens. The system is not converging.

question_text: A compound microscope consists of an objective lens of focal length 2.0 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 6.25 cm separated by a distance of 15 cm. How far from the objective should an object be placed in order to obtain the final image at the least distance of distinct vision (25 cm), and at infinity? What is the magnifying power of the microscope in each case?
question_html:
A compound microscope consists of an objective lens of focal length 2.0 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 6.25 cm separated by a distance of 15 cm. How far from the objective should an object be placed in order to obtain the final image at

  1. the least distance of distinct vision (25 cm), and
  2. at infinity?

What is the magnifying power of the microscope in each case?
solution_text: Given that, Focal length of the objective lens, f 1 = 2.0 cm Focal length of the eyepiece, f 2 = 6.25 cm Distance between the objective lens and the eyepiece, d = 15 cm Given that, Least distance of distinct vision, d' = 25 cm Image distance for the eyepiece, v 2 = -25 cm Object distance for the eyepiece = u 2 According to the lens formula, {tex}\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{u_{2}}=\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{u_{2}}=\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{-25}-\frac{1}{6.25}=\frac{-1-4}{25}=\frac{-5}{25}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} u 2 = -5cm Image distance for the objective lens, v 1 = d + u 2 = 15 - 5 = 10 cm Object distance for the objective lens = u 1 According to the lens formula, {tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{u_{1}}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{u_{1}}=\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{f_{1}}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1-5}{10}=\frac{-4}{10}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} u 1 = -2.5 cm Magnitude of the object distance, {tex}\left|u_{1}\right|=2.5 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} The magnifying power of a compound microscope is: {tex}m=\frac{v_{1}}{\left|u_{1}\right|}\left(1+\frac{d^{\prime}}{f_{2}}\right)=\frac{10}{2.5}\left(1+\frac{25}{6.25}\right)=4(1+4)=20{/tex} The final image is formed at infinity. Therefore, image distance for the eyepiece, v 2 = {tex}\infty{/tex} Object distance for the eyepiece = u 2 According to the lens formula, we have the relation, {tex}\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{u_{2}}=\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{u_{2}}=\frac{1}{6.25}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} u 2 = -6.25 cm Image distance for the objective lens, v 1 = d + u 2 = 15 - 6.25 = 8.75 cm Object distance for the objective lens = u 1 According to the lens formula, {tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{u_{1}}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{u_{1}}=\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{f_{1}}=\frac{1}{8.75}-\frac{1}{2.0}=\frac{2-8.75}{17.5}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad u_{1}=-\frac{17.5}{6.75}=-2.59 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} Magnitude of the object distance, {tex}\left|u_{1}\right|={/tex} 2.59 cm The magnifying power of a compound microscope is, {tex}m=\frac{v_{1}}{\left|u_{1}\right|}\left(\frac{d^{\prime}}{\left|u_{2}\right|}\right)=\frac{8.75}{2.59} \times \frac{25}{6.25}=13.51{/tex} Thus, the magnifying power of the microscope is 13.51
solution_html:
Given that,
Focal length of the objective lens, f1 = 2.0 cm
Focal length of the eyepiece, f2 = 6.25 cm
Distance between the objective lens and the eyepiece, d = 15 cm

  1. Given that, Least distance of distinct vision, d' = 25 cm
    Image distance for the eyepiece, v2 = -25 cm
    Object distance for the eyepiece = u2
    According to the lens formula,
    {tex}\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{u_{2}}=\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{u_{2}}=\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{-25}-\frac{1}{6.25}=\frac{-1-4}{25}=\frac{-5}{25}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} u2 = -5cm
    Image distance for the objective lens, v1 = d + u2 = 15 - 5 = 10 cm
    Object distance for the objective lens = u1
    According to the lens formula,
    {tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{u_{1}}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{u_{1}}=\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{f_{1}}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1-5}{10}=\frac{-4}{10}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} u1 = -2.5 cm
    Magnitude of the object distance, {tex}\left|u_{1}\right|=2.5 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
    The magnifying power of a compound microscope is:
    {tex}m=\frac{v_{1}}{\left|u_{1}\right|}\left(1+\frac{d^{\prime}}{f_{2}}\right)=\frac{10}{2.5}\left(1+\frac{25}{6.25}\right)=4(1+4)=20{/tex}
  2. The final image is formed at infinity.
    Therefore, image distance for the eyepiece, v2 = {tex}\infty{/tex}
    Object distance for the eyepiece = u2
    According to the lens formula, we have the relation,
    {tex}\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{u_{2}}=\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{u_{2}}=\frac{1}{6.25}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} u2 = -6.25 cm
    Image distance for the objective lens, v1 = d + u2 = 15 - 6.25 = 8.75 cm
    Object distance for the objective lens = u1
    According to the lens formula,
    {tex}\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{u_{1}}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{u_{1}}=\frac{1}{v_{1}}-\frac{1}{f_{1}}=\frac{1}{8.75}-\frac{1}{2.0}=\frac{2-8.75}{17.5}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad u_{1}=-\frac{17.5}{6.75}=-2.59 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
    Magnitude of the object distance, {tex}\left|u_{1}\right|={/tex} 2.59 cm
    The magnifying power of a compound microscope is,
    {tex}m=\frac{v_{1}}{\left|u_{1}\right|}\left(\frac{d^{\prime}}{\left|u_{2}\right|}\right)=\frac{8.75}{2.59} \times \frac{25}{6.25}=13.51{/tex}
    Thus, the magnifying power of the microscope is 13.51

question_text: A person with a normal near point (25 cm) using a compound microscope with objective of focal length 8.0 mm and an eye piece of focal length 2.5 cm can bring an object placed 9.0 mm from the objective in sharp focus. What is the separation between the two lenses? Calculate the magnifying power of the microscope.
question_html: A person with a normal near point (25 cm) using a compound microscope with objective of focal length 8.0 mm and an eye piece of focal length 2.5 cm can bring an object placed 9.0 mm from the objective in sharp focus. What is the separation between the two lenses? Calculate the magnifying power of the microscope.
solution_text: Here, u 0 = -0.9 cm, f 0 = 0.8 cm As, {tex}\frac{1}{{{v_0}}} - \frac{1}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_0}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{{{v_0}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_0}}} + \frac{1}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{0.8}} - \frac{1}{{0.9}} = \frac{1}{{7.2}}{/tex} or v 0 = 7.2 cm Now for the eyepiece, we have f e = 2.5 cm, v e = -25 cm, u e = ? {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{{{u_e}}} = \frac{1}{{{v_e}}} - \frac{1}{{{f_e}}} = - \frac{1}{{25}} - \frac{1}{{2.5}} = - \frac{{11}}{{25}}{/tex} or {tex}{u_e} = - \frac{{25}}{{11}} = - 2.27cm{/tex} Separation between the two lenses {tex} = {v_0} + \left| {{u_e}} \right|{/tex} = 7.2 + 2.27 = 9.47 cm Magnifying power, {tex}M = {M_0} \times {M_e}{/tex} {tex}M = \frac{{{v_0}}}{{{u_0}}}\left( {1 + \frac{D}{{{f_e}}}} \right) = \frac{{7.2}}{{0.9}}\left( {1 + \frac{{25}}{{2.5}}} \right){/tex} {tex} = 8 \times 11 = 88{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, u0 = -0.9 cm, f0 = 0.8 cm
As, {tex}\frac{1}{{{v_0}}} - \frac{1}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_0}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{{{v_0}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_0}}} + \frac{1}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{0.8}} - \frac{1}{{0.9}} = \frac{1}{{7.2}}{/tex}
or v0 = 7.2 cm
Now for the eyepiece, we have
fe = 2.5 cm, ve = -25 cm, ue = ?
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{{{u_e}}} = \frac{1}{{{v_e}}} - \frac{1}{{{f_e}}} = - \frac{1}{{25}} - \frac{1}{{2.5}} = - \frac{{11}}{{25}}{/tex}
or {tex}{u_e} = - \frac{{25}}{{11}} = - 2.27cm{/tex}
Separation between the two lenses
{tex} = {v_0} + \left| {{u_e}} \right|{/tex}
= 7.2 + 2.27 = 9.47 cm
Magnifying power, {tex}M = {M_0} \times {M_e}{/tex}
{tex}M = \frac{{{v_0}}}{{{u_0}}}\left( {1 + \frac{D}{{{f_e}}}} \right) = \frac{{7.2}}{{0.9}}\left( {1 + \frac{{25}}{{2.5}}} \right){/tex}
{tex} = 8 \times 11 = 88{/tex}

question_text: A small telescope has an objective lens of focal length 144 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 6.0 cm. What is the magnifying power of the telescope? What is the separation between the objective and the eyepiece?
question_html: A small telescope has an objective lens of focal length 144 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 6.0 cm. What is the magnifying power of the telescope? What is the separation between the objective and the eyepiece?
solution_text: Given, focal length of objective lens, f 0 = 144 cm Focal length of eyepiece, f e = 6 cm Magnifying power of the telescope in normal adjustment,(i.e. when the final image is formed at {tex}\infty{/tex} ) M = {tex} \frac { f _ { 0 } } { f _ { e } } = \frac { 144 } { 6 } = 24{/tex} Separation between lenses, L = f 0 + f e = 144 + 6 = 150 cm
solution_html:
Given, focal length of objective lens, f0 = 144 cm
Focal length of eyepiece, fe = 6 cm
Magnifying power of the telescope in normal adjustment,(i.e. when the final image is formed at {tex}\infty{/tex})
M = {tex} \frac { f _ { 0 } } { f _ { e } } = \frac { 144 } { 6 } = 24{/tex}
Separation between lenses,
L = f0 + fe = 144 + 6 = 150 cm

question_text: A giant refracting telescope has an objective lens of focal length 15 m. If an eyepiece of focal length 1.0 cm is used, what is the angular magnification of the telescope? If this telescope is used to view the moon, what is the diameter of the image of the moon formed by the objective lens? The diameter of the moon is {tex}3.48 \times 10 ^ { 6 } { m }{/tex} and the radius of lunar orbit is {tex}3.8 \times 10 ^ { 8 }{ m }{/tex} .
question_html:
1. A giant refracting telescope has an objective lens of focal length 15 m. If an eyepiece of focal length 1.0 cm is used, what is the angular magnification of the telescope?
  2. If this telescope is used to view the moon, what is the diameter of the image of the moon formed by the objective lens? The diameter of the moon is {tex}3.48 \times 10 ^ { 6 } { m }{/tex} and the radius of lunar orbit is {tex}3.8 \times 10 ^ { 8 }{ m }{/tex}.
solution_text: Let f o = focal length of the objective lens = 15 m = 1500 cm f e = focal length of the eye lens = 1 cm {tex}\because{/tex} Angular magnification of giant telescope is given by {tex}M = \left| \frac { f _ {0 } } { f _ { e } } \right| = \left| \frac { 1500 } { 1 } \right| = 1500{/tex} Diameter of the moon, d = 3.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m Radius of the lunar orbit, r 0 = 3.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m Let d’ be the diameter of the image of the moon formed by the objective lens. The angle subtended by the diameter of the moon is equal to the angle subtended by the image. The relation between the given variables is, {tex}\frac d{r_o}=\frac{d'}{f_o}{/tex} Thus, d' = {tex}\frac{3.48\times10^6\times15}{3.8\times10^8}{/tex} = 0.1373 m = 13.73 cm Hence the diameter of the image of the moon formed by the telescope is 13.73 cm.
solution_html:
1. Let fo = focal length of the objective lens = 15 m = 1500 cm
    fe = focal length of the eye lens = 1 cm
    {tex}\because{/tex} Angular magnification of giant telescope is given by
    {tex}M = \left| \frac { f _ {0 } } { f _ { e } } \right| = \left| \frac { 1500 } { 1 } \right| = 1500{/tex}
  2. Diameter of the moon, d = 3.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m
    Radius of the lunar orbit, r0 = 3.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m
    Let d’ be the diameter of the image of the moon formed by the objective lens. The angle subtended by the diameter of the moon is equal to the angle subtended by the image.
    The relation between the given variables is,
    {tex}\frac d{r_o}=\frac{d'}{f_o}{/tex}
    Thus, d' = {tex}\frac{3.48\times10^6\times15}{3.8\times10^8}{/tex} = 0.1373 m = 13.73 cm
    Hence the diameter of the image of the moon formed by the telescope is 13.73 cm.

question_text: Use the mirror equation to deduce that: an object placed between f and 2f of a concave mirror produces a real image beyond 2f. a convex mirror always produces a virtual image independent of the location of the object. the virtual image produced by a convex mirror is always diminished in size and is located between the focus and the pole. an object placed between the pole and focus of a concave mirror produces a virtual and enlarged image. [ Note: This exercise helps you deduce algebraically properties of images that one obtains from explicit ray diagrams.]
question_html:
Use the mirror equation to deduce that:

  1. an object placed between f and 2f of a concave mirror produces a real image beyond 2f.
  2. a convex mirror always produces a virtual image independent of the location of the object.
  3. the virtual image produced by a convex mirror is always diminished in size and is located between the focus and the pole.
  4. an object placed between the pole and focus of a concave mirror produces a virtual and enlarged image.
    [Note: This exercise helps you deduce algebraically properties of images that one obtains from explicit ray diagrams.]
solution_text: The mirror formula is: {tex}\frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u}{/tex} Now for a concave mirror, f < 0 and for an object on the left u < 0. 2 f < u < f or {tex}\frac{1}{{2f}} > \frac{1}{u} > \frac{1}{f}{/tex} or {tex} - \frac{1}{{2f}} < - \frac{1}{u} < - \frac{1}{f}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{{2f}} < \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u} < \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{f}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{{2f}} < \frac{1}{v} < 0{/tex} This implies that v < 0 so that real image is formed on left. Also the above inequality implies that 2 f > v or |2 f| > |v| [ {tex}\because {/tex} 2f and v are -ve] i.e. real image is formed beyond 2f. Now, for convex mirror, f > 0 and for an object of left, u < 0. From mirror formula {tex}\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} > 0{/tex} or v > 0 This shows that whatever be the value of u, a convex mirror form a virtual image on the right. For convex mirror f > 0 and for an object on left u < 0, so from mirror formula, {tex}\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u}{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} v is +ve, u is -ve] {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} > \frac{1}{f}{/tex} or {tex}v < f{/tex} ( {tex}\therefore - \frac{1}{u}{/tex} is a +ve quantity) This shows that the image is located between the pole and the focus of the mirror. Also from the mirror formula, {tex}\frac{1}{v} > - \frac{1}{u}\left( {\because \frac{1}{f} > 0} \right){/tex} Multiply v to both sides, {tex}\therefore \frac{v}{v} > - \frac{u}{v}{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} v is +ve] 1 > m ( {tex}\because {/tex} u < 0) Magnitude of magnification, {tex}m = \frac{v}{{|u|}} < 1{/tex} So the image is diminished in size. From the mirror formula, for a concave mirror, f < 0 and for an object located between the pole and focus of a concave mirror, f < u < 0 {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{f} > \frac{1}{u}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u} > 0{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{v} > 0{/tex} or {tex}v > 0\left( {\therefore \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u}} \right){/tex} i.e. a virtual image is formed on the right. Also, {tex}\frac{1}{v} < \frac{1}{{|u|}}{/tex} or v > |u| {tex}\therefore |m| = \frac{v}{{|u|}} > 1{/tex} i.e., Image is enlarged.
solution_html:
1. The mirror formula is:
    {tex}\frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u}{/tex}
    Now for a concave mirror, f < 0 and for an object on the left u < 0.
    2 f < u < f
    or {tex}\frac{1}{{2f}} > \frac{1}{u} > \frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    or {tex} - \frac{1}{{2f}} < - \frac{1}{u} < - \frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    or {tex}\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{{2f}} < \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u} < \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    or {tex}\frac{1}{{2f}} < \frac{1}{v} < 0{/tex}
    This implies that v < 0 so that real image is formed on left. Also the above inequality implies that
    2 f > v
    or |2 f| > |v| [{tex}\because {/tex} 2f and v are -ve]
    i.e. real image is formed beyond 2f.
  2. Now, for convex mirror, f > 0 and for an object of left, u < 0.
    From mirror formula
    {tex}\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u}{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} > 0{/tex} or v > 0
    This shows that whatever be the value of u, a convex mirror form a virtual image on the right.
  3. For convex mirror f > 0 and for an object on left u < 0, so from mirror formula,
    {tex}\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u}{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} v is +ve, u is -ve]
    {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} > \frac{1}{f}{/tex} or {tex}v < f{/tex} ({tex}\therefore - \frac{1}{u}{/tex}  is a +ve quantity)
    This shows that the image is located between the pole and the focus of the mirror. Also from the mirror formula,
    {tex}\frac{1}{v} > - \frac{1}{u}\left( {\because \frac{1}{f} > 0} \right){/tex}
    Multiply v to both sides,
    {tex}\therefore \frac{v}{v} > - \frac{u}{v}{/tex}  [ {tex}\because {/tex}v is +ve]
    1 > m ( {tex}\because {/tex} u < 0)
    Magnitude of magnification, {tex}m = \frac{v}{{|u|}} < 1{/tex}
    So the image is diminished in size.
  4. From the mirror formula, for a concave mirror, f < 0 and for an object located between the pole and focus of a concave mirror,
    f < u < 0
    {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{f} > \frac{1}{u}{/tex}
    or {tex}\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u} > 0{/tex}
    or {tex}\frac{1}{v} > 0{/tex} or {tex}v > 0\left( {\therefore \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{u}} \right){/tex}
    i.e. a virtual image is formed on the right.
    Also, {tex}\frac{1}{v} < \frac{1}{{|u|}}{/tex} or v > |u|
    {tex}\therefore |m| = \frac{v}{{|u|}} > 1{/tex} i.e., Image is enlarged.

question_text: A small pin fixed on a table top is viewed from above from a distance of 50 cm. By what distance would the pin appear to be raised if it is viewed from the same point through a 15 cm thick glass slab held parallel to the table? Refracting index of glass = 1.5 . Does the answer depend on the location of the slab?
question_html: A small pin fixed on a table top is viewed from above from a distance of 50 cm. By what distance would the pin appear to be raised if it is viewed from the same point through a 15 cm thick glass slab held parallel to the table? Refracting index of glass = 1.5 . Does the answer depend on the location of the slab?
solution_text: Given: real depth = 15 cm, μ = 1.5 Let the apparent depth be x. Now, μ = {tex}\frac {15}x{/tex} Thus, x = {tex}\frac {15}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac {15}{1.5}{/tex} = 10cm Distance through which the pin appears to be raised is = 15 −10 = 5 cm And it does not depend on the location of the slab.
solution_html:
Given: real depth = 15 cm, μ = 1.5
Let the apparent depth be x.
Now, μ = {tex}\frac {15}x{/tex}
Thus, x = {tex}\frac {15}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac {15}{1.5}{/tex} = 10cm
Distance through which the pin appears to be raised is = 15 −10 = 5 cm
And it does not depend on the location of the slab.

question_text: Figure shows a cross-section of a light pipe made of a glass fibre of refractive index 1.68. The outer covering of the pipe is made of a material of refractive index 1.44. What is the range of the angles of the incident rays with the axis of the pipe for which total reflections inside the pipe take place as shown in the figure. What is the answer, if there is no outer covering of the pipe?
question_html:
1. Figure shows a cross-section of a light pipe made of a glass fibre of refractive index 1.68. The outer covering of the pipe is made of a material of refractive index 1.44. What is the range of the angles of the incident rays with the axis of the pipe for which total reflections inside the pipe take place as shown in the figure.
  2. What is the answer, if there is no outer covering of the pipe?
solution_text: The working of an optical fibre is based on the phenomena of total internal reflection. Given, refractive index of the glass fibre with respect to air, {tex}\mu _ { 2 } = \;^{ a } \mu _ { g }{/tex} = 1.68 Refractive index of the outer coating material with respect to air, {tex}\mu _ { 1 } = \;^ { a } \mu _ { \text { outer } }{/tex} = 1.44 Let the critical angle be i c So, {tex}\mu = \frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { \sin i _ { c } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin i _ { c } = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1.44 } { 1.68 } = 0.8571{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow i _ { c } = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 0.8571 ) \approx 59 ^ { \circ }{/tex} The total internal reflection will take place when the angle of incidence i will be greater than the critical angle i c , i.e. i > i c = 59° or when angle of refraction, r < r max where, r max = 90° - i c = 90° - 59° = 31° So, {tex}^ { a } \mu _ { g } = \frac { \sin i _ { \max } } { \sin r _ { \max } } = 1.68{/tex} or, sin i max = 1.68 sin 31° = 1.68 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5150 = 0.8652 or i max = sin -1 (0.8652) = 60° Thus, all the rays which are incident in the range 0 < i < 60°, will suffer total internal reflection in the pipe (but i {tex}\ne{/tex} 0). If the outer covering of the pipe is not present, then, Refractive index of the outer pipe, μ 1 = Refractive index of air = 1 For the angle of incidence i = 90°, we can write Snell’s law at the air−pipe interface as: {tex}\frac{\sin\;i}{\sin\;r}{/tex} = μ where, i = angle of incidence, r = angle of refraction, {tex}\frac{\sin\;i}{\sin\;r}{/tex} = 1.68 sin r = {tex}\frac{\sin90}{1.68}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1.68}{/tex} = 0.59 ⇒ r = 36.5° Now, i ' = 90° - r ⇒ i′ = 90°− 36.5° ⇒ i ' = 53.5° Since i’ > r, all incident rays will suffer total internal reflection.
solution_html:
1. The working of an optical fibre is based on the phenomena of total internal reflection.
    Given, refractive index of the glass fibre with respect to air,
    {tex}\mu _ { 2 } = \;^{ a } \mu _ { g }{/tex}= 1.68
    Refractive index of the outer coating material with respect to air,
    {tex}\mu _ { 1 } = \;^ { a } \mu _ { \text { outer } }{/tex}= 1.44
    Let the critical angle be ic
    So, {tex}\mu = \frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { \sin i _ { c } }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \sin i _ { c } = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1.44 } { 1.68 } = 0.8571{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow i _ { c } = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 0.8571 ) \approx 59 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
    The total internal reflection will take place when the angle of incidence i will be greater than the critical angle ic, i.e. i > ic = 59° or when angle of refraction, r < rmax
    where, rmax = 90° - ic = 90° - 59° = 31°
    So, {tex}^ { a } \mu _ { g } = \frac { \sin i _ { \max } } { \sin r _ { \max } } = 1.68{/tex}
    or, sin imax = 1.68 sin 31° = 1.68 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5150 = 0.8652
    or imax  = sin-1 (0.8652) = 60°
    Thus, all the rays which are incident in the range 0 < i < 60°, will suffer total internal reflection in the pipe (but i {tex}\ne{/tex} 0).

  2. If the outer covering of the pipe is not present, then,
    Refractive index of the outer pipe, μ1 = Refractive index of air = 1
    For the angle of incidence i = 90°, we can write Snell’s law at the air−pipe interface as:
    {tex}\frac{\sin\;i}{\sin\;r}{/tex} = μ
    where, i = angle of incidence, r = angle of refraction, 
    {tex}\frac{\sin\;i}{\sin\;r}{/tex} = 1.68
    sin r = {tex}\frac{\sin90}{1.68}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{1.68}{/tex} = 0.59
    ⇒ r = 36.5°
    Now, i' = 90° - r
    ⇒ i′ = 90°− 36.5°
    ⇒ i' = 53.5°
    Since i’ > r, all incident rays will suffer total internal reflection.

question_text: The image of a small electric bulb fixed on the wall of a room is to be obtained on the opposite wall 3m away by means of a large convex lens. What is the maximum possible focal length of the lens required for the purpose?
question_html: The image of a small electric bulb fixed on the wall of a room is to be obtained on the opposite wall 3m away by means of a large convex lens. What is the maximum possible focal length of the lens required for the purpose?
solution_text: Let the object be placed x m in front of the lens the distance of the image from the lens is (3 - x)m Applying lens formula, {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{(3-x)}-\frac{1}{-x}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{(3-x)}+\frac{1}{x}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{x+3-x}{x(3-x)}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} or 3f = 3x - x 2 So, x 2 - 3x + 3f = 0 Now {tex}x=\frac{+3 \pm \sqrt{9-4 \times(3 f)}}{2}{/tex} or {tex}x=\frac{+3 \pm \sqrt{9-12 f}}{2}{/tex} Condition for the image to be obtained on the screen, i.e.m real image. 9 - 12f {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 or 9 {tex}\geq{/tex} 12 f or f {tex}\leq{/tex} 0.75 m. So, the maximum focal length is 0.75 m.
solution_html:
Let the object be placed x m in front of the lens the distance of the image from the lens is (3 - x)m
Applying lens formula, {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{(3-x)}-\frac{1}{-x}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{(3-x)}+\frac{1}{x}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{x+3-x}{x(3-x)}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} or 3f = 3x - x2
So, x2 - 3x + 3f = 0
Now {tex}x=\frac{+3 \pm \sqrt{9-4 \times(3 f)}}{2}{/tex}
or {tex}x=\frac{+3 \pm \sqrt{9-12 f}}{2}{/tex}
Condition for the image to be obtained on the screen, i.e.m real image. 9 - 12f {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 or 9 {tex}\geq{/tex} 12 f or f {tex}\leq{/tex} 0.75 m. So, the maximum focal length is 0.75 m.

question_text: A screen is placed 90 cm from an object. The image of the object on the screen is formed by a convex lens at two different locations separated by 20 cm. Determine the focal length of the lens.
question_html: A screen is placed 90 cm from an object. The image of the object on the screen is formed by a convex lens at two different locations separated by 20 cm. Determine the focal length of the lens.
solution_text: Here, distance between object and screen, D = 90 cm Distance between two locations of convex lens, d = 20 cm Since, {tex}f = \frac{{{D^2} - {d^2}}}{{4D}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore f = \frac{{{{(90)}^2} - {{(20)}^2}}}{{4 \times 90}} = \frac{{(90 + 20)(90 - 20)}}{{360}} = \frac{{110 \times 7}}{{36}}{/tex} f = 21.4 cm
solution_html:
Here, distance between object and screen, D = 90 cm
Distance between two locations of convex lens, d = 20 cm
Since, {tex}f = \frac{{{D^2} - {d^2}}}{{4D}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore f = \frac{{{{(90)}^2} - {{(20)}^2}}}{{4 \times 90}} = \frac{{(90 + 20)(90 - 20)}}{{360}} = \frac{{110 \times 7}}{{36}}{/tex}
f = 21.4 cm

question_text: Determine the 'effective focal length' of the combination of the two lenses having focal lengths 30 cm and -20cm if they are placed 8.0 cm apart with their principal axes coincident. Does the answer depend on which side of the combination a beam of parallel light is incident? Is the notion of effective focal length of this system useful at all?
question_html: Determine the 'effective focal length' of the combination of the two lenses having focal lengths 30 cm and -20cm if they are placed 8.0 cm apart with their principal axes coincident. Does the answer depend on which side of the combination a beam of parallel light is incident? Is the notion of effective focal length of this system useful at all?
solution_text: Here, f 1 = 30 cm, f 2 = -20 cm, d = 8.0 cm Let a parallel beam be incident on the convex lens first. If second lens were absent, then {tex}\therefore u _ { 1 } = \infty{/tex} and f 1 = 30 cm As {tex}\frac { 1 } { v _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { u _ { 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 1 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { 1 } { v _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { \infty } = \frac { 1 } { 30 }{/tex} or v 1 = 30 cm This image would now act as virtual object for second lens. {tex}\therefore{/tex} u 2 = +(30 - 8) = +22 cm f 2 = -20 cm Since, {tex}\frac { 1 } { v _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { u _ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac { 1 } { v _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { - 20 } + \frac { 1 } { 22 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 11 + 10 } { 220 } = \frac { - 1 } { 220 }{/tex} v 2 = -220 cm {tex}\therefore{/tex} Parallel incident beam would appear to diverge from a point 220 - 4 = 216 cm from the centre of the two lens system. Assume that a parallel beam of light from the left is incident first on the concave lens. {tex}\therefore u _ { 1 } = - \infty{/tex} , f 1 = -20 cm As {tex}\frac { 1 } { v _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { u _ { 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 1 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { 1 } { v _ { 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { \mathrm { f } _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { \mathrm { u } _ { 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { - 20 } + \frac { 1 } { - \infty } = - \frac { 1 } { 20 }{/tex} v 1 = -20cm This image acts as a real object for the second lens u 2 = -(20 + 8) = -28cm, f 2 = 30 cm Since, {tex}\frac { 1 } { v _ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { u _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { 1 } { v _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { u _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 30 } - \frac { 1 } { 28 } = \frac { 14 - 15 } { 420 }{/tex} v 2 = -420 cm The parallel beam appears to diverge from a point 420 - 4 = 416 cm, on the left of the centre of the two lens system. We finally conclude that the answer depends on the side of the lens system where the parallel beam is incident. Therefore, the notion of effective focal length does not seem to be meaningful here.
solution_html:
Here, f1 = 30 cm, f2 = -20 cm, d = 8.0 cm
Let a parallel beam be incident on the convex lens first. If second lens were absent, then
{tex}\therefore u _ { 1 } = \infty{/tex} and f1 = 30 cm
As {tex}\frac { 1 } { v _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { u _ { 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 1 } }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac { 1 } { v _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { \infty } = \frac { 1 } { 30 }{/tex}
or v1 = 30 cm
This image would now act as virtual object for second lens.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} u2 = +(30 - 8) = +22 cm
f2 = -20 cm
Since, {tex}\frac { 1 } { v _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { u _ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \frac { 1 } { v _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { - 20 } + \frac { 1 } { 22 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - 11 + 10 } { 220 } = \frac { - 1 } { 220 }{/tex}
v2 = -220 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Parallel incident beam would appear to diverge from a point 220 - 4 = 216 cm from the centre of the two lens system.

Assume that a parallel beam of light from the left is incident first on the concave lens.
{tex}\therefore u _ { 1 } = - \infty{/tex}, f1 = -20 cm
As {tex}\frac { 1 } { v _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { u _ { 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 1 } }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac { 1 } { v _ { 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { \mathrm { f } _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { \mathrm { u } _ { 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { - 20 } + \frac { 1 } { - \infty } = - \frac { 1 } { 20 }{/tex}
v1 = -20cm
This image acts as a real object for the second lens
u2 = -(20 + 8) = -28cm, f2 = 30 cm
Since, {tex}\frac { 1 } { v _ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { u _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac { 1 } { v _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { u _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 30 } - \frac { 1 } { 28 } = \frac { 14 - 15 } { 420 }{/tex}
v2 = -420 cm
The parallel beam appears to diverge from a point 420 - 4 = 416 cm, on the left of the centre of the two lens system.
We finally conclude that the answer depends on the side of the lens system where the parallel beam is incident. Therefore, the notion of effective focal length does not seem to be meaningful here.

question_text: An object 1.5 cm in size is placed on the side of the convex lens in the arrangement (a) above. The distance between the object and the convex lens is 40 cm. Determine the magnification produced by the two-lens system and the size of the image.
question_html: An object 1.5 cm in size is placed on the side of the convex lens in the arrangement (a) above. The distance between the object and the convex lens is 40 cm. Determine the magnification produced by the two-lens system and the size of the image.
solution_text: Given Object height = 1.5 cm Object distance from convex lens = -40cm According to lens formula {tex}\frac{1}{v_{\text {fromconvex }}}=\frac{1}{f_{\text {convex }}}+\frac{1}{u_{\text {fromconvex }}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_{\text {fromconvex }}}=\frac{1}{30}+\frac{1}{-40}=\frac{1}{120}{/tex} v fromconvex = 120 Magnification due to convex lens: {tex}m_{\text {convex }}=-\frac{v}{u}=-\frac{120}{-40}=3{/tex} The image of convex lens will act as an object for concave lens, So, {tex}\frac{1}{v_{\text {fromconcave }}}=\frac{1}{f_{\text {concave }}}+\frac{1}{u_{\text {fromconcave }}}{/tex} u concave = 120 - 8 = 112 {tex}\frac{1}{v_{\text {fromconcave }}}=\frac{1}{-20}+\frac{1}{112}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v_{\text {fromconcave }}}=-\frac{92}{2240}{/tex} {tex}v_{\text {fromconcave }}=\frac{-2240}{92}{/tex} Magnification due to concave lens: {tex}m_{\text {combined }}=m_{\text {convex }} * m_{\text {concave }}{/tex} {tex}m_{\text {combined }}=3 * \frac{20}{92}=0.652{/tex} Hence height of the image = m combined * h = 0.652 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5 = 0.98cm Hence height of image is 0.98cm.
solution_html:
Given
Object height = 1.5 cm
Object distance from convex lens = -40cm
According to lens formula
{tex}\frac{1}{v_{\text {fromconvex }}}=\frac{1}{f_{\text {convex }}}+\frac{1}{u_{\text {fromconvex }}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{v_{\text {fromconvex }}}=\frac{1}{30}+\frac{1}{-40}=\frac{1}{120}{/tex}
vfromconvex = 120
Magnification due to convex lens:
{tex}m_{\text {convex }}=-\frac{v}{u}=-\frac{120}{-40}=3{/tex}
The image of convex lens will act as an object for concave lens,
So,
{tex}\frac{1}{v_{\text {fromconcave }}}=\frac{1}{f_{\text {concave }}}+\frac{1}{u_{\text {fromconcave }}}{/tex}
uconcave = 120 - 8 = 112
{tex}\frac{1}{v_{\text {fromconcave }}}=\frac{1}{-20}+\frac{1}{112}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{v_{\text {fromconcave }}}=-\frac{92}{2240}{/tex}
{tex}v_{\text {fromconcave }}=\frac{-2240}{92}{/tex}
Magnification due to concave lens:
{tex}m_{\text {combined }}=m_{\text {convex }} * m_{\text {concave }}{/tex}
{tex}m_{\text {combined }}=3 * \frac{20}{92}=0.652{/tex}
Hence height of the image = mcombined * h
= 0.652 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5 = 0.98cm
Hence height of image is 0.98cm.

question_text: At what angle should a ray of light be incident on the face of a prism of refracting angle 60° so that it just suffers total internal reflection at the other face? The refractive index of the material of the prism is 1.524
question_html: At what angle should a ray of light be incident on the face of a prism of refracting angle 60° so that it just suffers total internal reflection at the other face? The refractive index of the material of the prism is 1.524
solution_text: The beam should be incident at critical angle or more than critical angle, for total internal reflection at second surface of the prism. Let us first find critical angle for air glass interface. We know {tex}\sin C=\frac{1}{^{a} \mu_{g}}{/tex} {tex}C=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{a \mu_{g}}\right)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{1.524}\right){/tex} Critical angle C = 41 o Now we can calculate 'r', as the refracted ray in the prism is incident on the second face at critical angle i c. thus, 60 o + (90 o - r) + (90 o - C) = 180 o or , r = 19 o Using Snell's law, required angle of incidence i at first surface can be calculated. {tex}^{a} \mu_{g}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} or {tex}1.524=\frac{\sin i}{\sin 19^{\circ}}{/tex} sin i = 1.524 (sin 19 o ) sin i = 1.524 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.3256 {tex}\Rightarrow i \cong 29.75^{\circ}{/tex}
solution_html:
The beam should be incident at critical angle or more than critical angle, for total internal reflection at second surface of the prism.

Let us first find critical angle for air glass interface.
We know
{tex}\sin C=\frac{1}{^{a} \mu_{g}}{/tex}
{tex}C=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{a \mu_{g}}\right)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{1.524}\right){/tex}
Critical angle C = 41o
Now we can calculate 'r', as the refracted ray in the prism is incident on the second face at critical angle ic. thus,
60o + (90o - r) + (90o - C) = 180o
or , r = 19o
Using Snell's law, required angle of incidence i at first surface can be calculated.
{tex}^{a} \mu_{g}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} or {tex}1.524=\frac{\sin i}{\sin 19^{\circ}}{/tex}
sin i = 1.524 (sin 19o)

 sin i = 1.524 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.3256 {tex}\Rightarrow i \cong 29.75^{\circ}{/tex}

question_text: A card sheet divided into squares each of size 1 mm 2 is being viewed at a distance of 9 cm through a magnifying glass (a converging lens of focal length 9 cm) held close to the eye. What is the magnification produced by the lens? How much is the area of each square in the virtual image? What is the angular magnification (magnifying power) of the lens? Is the magnification in (a) equal to the magnifying power in (b)? Explain.
question_html:
A card sheet divided into squares each of size 1 mm2 is being viewed at a distance of 9 cm through a magnifying glass (a converging lens of focal length 9 cm) held close to the eye.

  1. What is the magnification produced by the lens? How much is the area of each square in the virtual image?
  2. What is the angular magnification (magnifying power) of the lens?
  3. Is the magnification in (a) equal to the magnifying power in (b)? Explain.
solution_text: Area of each square, A = 1 mm 2 Object distance, u = -9 cm Focal length of a converging lens, f = 9 cm For image distance v, the lens formula can be written as; {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{9}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{9}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{81}{/tex} {tex}\therefore v=-81 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} Magnification is given by, {tex}m=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-81}{-9}=9{/tex} Area of each square in the virtual image = (10) 2 A = 10 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 100 mm 2 = 1 cm 2 Magnifying power of the lens = {tex}\frac{d}{|u|}=\frac{25}{9}{/tex} = 2.8 No, magnification of an image by a lens and angular magnification (or magnifying power) of an optical instrument are two separate things. The magnification in (a) is not the same as the magnifying power in (b) The magnification magnitude is {tex}\left(\left|\frac{v}{u}\right|\right){/tex} and the magnifying power is {tex}\left(\frac{d}{|u|}\right){/tex} . The two quantities will be equal when the images is formed at the near point (25 cm).
solution_html:
1. Area of each square, A = 1 mm2
    Object distance, u = -9 cm
    Focal length of a converging lens, f = 9 cm
    For image distance v, the lens formula can be written as;
    {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{9}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{9}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{81}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore v=-81 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
    Magnification is given by, {tex}m=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-81}{-9}=9{/tex}
    Area of each square in the virtual image = (10)2A = 102 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 100 mm2 = 1 cm2
  2. Magnifying power of the lens = {tex}\frac{d}{|u|}=\frac{25}{9}{/tex} = 2.8
  3. No, magnification of an image by a lens and angular magnification (or magnifying power) of an optical instrument are two separate things. The magnification in (a) is not the same as the magnifying power in (b) The magnification magnitude is {tex}\left(\left|\frac{v}{u}\right|\right){/tex} and the magnifying power is {tex}\left(\frac{d}{|u|}\right){/tex}. The two quantities will be equal when the images is formed at the near point (25 cm).

question_text: At what distance should the lens be held in order to view the squares distinctly with the maximum possible magnifying power? What is the magnification in this case? Is the magnification equal to the magnifying power in this case? Explain.
question_html:
1. At what distance should the lens be held in order to view the squares distinctly with the maximum possible magnifying power?
  2. What is the magnification in this case?
  3. Is the magnification equal to the magnifying power in this case? Explain.
solution_text: Maximum magnifying power is obtained when the image is at the near point (25 cm). Thus, v = -25cm, f = +10 cm, u = ? As, {tex}\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{ - 25}} - \frac{1}{{10}} = \frac{{ - 2 - 5}}{{50}} = \frac{{ - 7}}{{50}}{/tex} or {tex}u = - \frac{{50}}{7} = - 7.14cm{/tex} So the lens should be held 7.14 cm away from the figure. Magnitude of magnification is {tex}m = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{{25}}{{\frac{{50}}{7}}} = 3.5{/tex} Magnifying power, {tex}M = \frac{D}{u} = \frac{{25}}{{\frac{{50}}{7}}} = 3.5{/tex} Yes, the magnifying power is equal to the magnitude of magnification because the image is formed at the least distance of distinct vision.
solution_html:
1. Maximum magnifying power is obtained when the image is at the near point (25 cm). Thus,
    v = -25cm, f = +10 cm, u = ?
    As, {tex}\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{1}{{ - 25}} - \frac{1}{{10}} = \frac{{ - 2 - 5}}{{50}} = \frac{{ - 7}}{{50}}{/tex}
    or {tex}u = - \frac{{50}}{7} = - 7.14cm{/tex}
    So the lens should be held 7.14 cm away from the figure.
  2. Magnitude of magnification is
    {tex}m = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{{25}}{{\frac{{50}}{7}}} = 3.5{/tex}
  3. Magnifying power, {tex}M = \frac{D}{u} = \frac{{25}}{{\frac{{50}}{7}}} = 3.5{/tex}
    Yes, the magnifying power is equal to the magnitude of magnification because the image is formed at the least distance of distinct vision.

question_text: What should be the distance between the object in Exercise 9.23 and the magnifying glass if the virtual image of each square in the figure is to have an area of 6.25 mm 2 . Would you be able to see the squares distinctly with your eyes very close to the magnifier? [Note: Exercises 9.22 to 9.24 will help you clearly understand the difference between magnification in absolute size and the angular magnification (or magnifying power) of an instrument.]
question_html:
What should be the distance between the object in Exercise 9.23 and the magnifying glass if the virtual image of each square in the figure is to have an area of 6.25 mm2. Would you be able to see the squares distinctly with your eyes very close to the magnifier?
[Note: Exercises 9.22 to 9.24 will help you clearly understand the difference between magnification in absolute size and the angular magnification (or magnifying power) of an instrument.]
solution_text: Area of the virtual image of each square, A = 6.25 mm 2 Area of each square, A 0 = 1 mm 2 Hence, the linear magnification of the object can be calculated as: {tex}\mathrm{m}=\sqrt{\frac{A}{A_{0}}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{\frac{6.25}{1}}=2.5{/tex} But m {tex}=\frac{\text { Image distance }(\mathrm{v})}{\text { Object distnace }(\mathrm{u})}{/tex} {tex}\therefore v=mu{/tex} = 2.5 u ..... (i) Focal length of the magnifying glass, f = 10 cm According to the lens formula, we have the relation; {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{10}=\frac{1}{2.5u} -\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{u}\left(\frac{1}{2.5}-\frac{1}{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{u}\left(\frac{1-2.5}{2.5}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore u=-\frac{1.5 \times 10}{2.5}=-6 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} And v = 2.5 u {tex}=2.5 \times 6=-15 \mathrm{cm}{/tex} The virtual image is formed at a distance of 15 cm, which is less than the near point (i.e., 25 cm) of a normal eye. Hence, the squares cannot be seen distinctly with your eyes very close to the magnifier.
solution_html:
Area of the virtual image of each square, A = 6.25 mm2
Area of each square, A0 = 1 mm2
Hence, the linear magnification of the object can be calculated as:
{tex}\mathrm{m}=\sqrt{\frac{A}{A_{0}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\frac{6.25}{1}}=2.5{/tex}
But m {tex}=\frac{\text { Image distance }(\mathrm{v})}{\text { Object distnace }(\mathrm{u})}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore v=mu{/tex}
= 2.5 u ..... (i)
Focal length of the magnifying glass, f = 10 cm
According to the lens formula, we have the relation;
{tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{10}=\frac{1}{2.5u} -\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{u}\left(\frac{1}{2.5}-\frac{1}{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{u}\left(\frac{1-2.5}{2.5}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore u=-\frac{1.5 \times 10}{2.5}=-6 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
And v = 2.5 u
{tex}=2.5 \times 6=-15 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
The virtual image is formed at a distance of 15 cm, which is less than the near point (i.e., 25 cm) of a normal eye. Hence, the squares cannot be seen distinctly with your eyes very close to the magnifier.

question_text: The angle subtended at the eye by an object is equal to the angle subtended at the eye by the virtual image produced by a magnifying glass. In what sense then does a magnifying glass provide angular magnification?
question_html: The angle subtended at the eye by an object is equal to the angle subtended at the eye by the virtual image produced by a magnifying glass. In what sense then does a magnifying glass provide angular magnification?
solution_text: Angular size of the image is equal to the angular size of the object. Hence by using magnifying glass, we keep the object closer to the eye at 25 cm, its normal position with out use of glass. Hence the closer object has larger angular size than the same object at 25 cm. It is in this sense the magnification is achieved

question_text: In viewing through a magnifying glass, one usually positions one's eye very close to the lens. Does angular magnification change if the eye is moved back?
question_html: In viewing through a magnifying glass, one usually positions one's eye very close to the lens. Does angular magnification change if the eye is moved back?
solution_text: Yes, the angular magnification decreases a little because the angle subtended at the eye by the image is then slightly less than the angle subtended by the image at the lens. The angle subtended by the object at the eye is also less than that subtended by the object at the lens. However, this decrease is very small as compared to the case of image. Also, when the eye is separated from the lens the angles subtended at the eye by the object and its image are not equal. It may further be noted that if the image is a very large distance away, then the effect, on magnification, of moving the eye shall be negligible.

question_text: Magnifying power of a simple microscope is inversely proportional to the focal length of the lens. What then stops us from using a convex lens of smaller and smaller focal length and achieving greater and greater magnifying power?
question_html: Magnifying power of a simple microscope is inversely proportional to the focal length of the lens. What then stops us from using a convex lens of smaller and smaller focal length and achieving greater and greater magnifying power?
solution_text: Firstly, grinding lenses of very small focal lengths are not easy. More importantly, if we decrease focal length, aberrations become more pronounced. So, in practice, we can't get a magnifying power of more than 3 or so with a simple convex lens. However, using an aberration corrected lens system, one can increase this limit by a factor of 10 or so.

question_text: Why must both the objective and eyepiece of a compound microscope have short focal lengths?
question_html: Why must both the objective and eyepiece of a compound microscope have short focal lengths?
solution_text: In a compound microscope, magnifying power = {tex}(\frac L{f_o})(\frac D{f_e}){/tex} Hence, to increase magnifying power, both f o and f e must be small.
solution_html:
In a compound microscope,
magnifying power = {tex}(\frac L{f_o})(\frac D{f_e}){/tex}
Hence, to increase magnifying power, both fo and fe must be small.

subject: Physics
question_text: When viewing through a compound microscope, our eyes should be positioned not on the eyepiece but a short distance away from it for best viewing. Why? How much should be that short distance between the eye and eyepiece?
question_html: When viewing through a compound microscope, our eyes should be positioned not on the eyepiece but a short distance away from it for best viewing. Why? How much should be that short distance between the eye and eyepiece?
solution_text: The image of the objective lens in the eyepiece is known as the 'eyering'. All the rays from the object refracted by the objective go through the eye ring. Therefore, it is an ideal position for our eyes for viewing. If we place our eyes too close to the eyepiece, we shall not collect much of the light and also reduce our field of view. If we position our eyes on the eye ring and the area of the pupil of our eye is greater or equal to the area of the eyering, our eyes will collect all the light refracted by the objective. The precise location of the eye ring naturally depends on the separation between the objective and the eyepiece and the focal length of the eyepiece. When we view through a microscope by placing our eyes on one end, the ideal distance between the eye and the eyepiece is usually built in the design of the instrument.
