question_text: An angular magnification of 30X is desired using an objective of focal length 1.25 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 5 cm. How will you set up the compound microscope?
question_html: An angular magnification of 30X is desired using an objective of focal length 1.25 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 5 cm. How will you set up the compound microscope?
solution_text: In normal adjustment, image is formed at least distance of distinct vision, d = 25 cm Angular magnification of eyepiece {tex} = \left( {1 + \frac{D}{{{f_e}}}} \right){/tex} {tex} = \left( {1 + \frac{{25}}{5}} \right) = 6{/tex} Since the total magnification is 30, magnification of objective lens, {tex}m = \frac{{30}}{6} = 5{/tex} Now, {tex} m = - \frac{{{v_0}}}{{{u_0}}} = 5{/tex} or {tex}{v_0} = -5{u_0}{/tex} As {tex}\frac{1}{{{v_0}}} - \frac{1}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_0}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{1}{{ - 5{u_0}}} - \frac{1}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{1.25}}{/tex} {tex} - \frac{6}{{5{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{1.25}}{/tex} {tex}{u_0} = - \frac{{6 \times 1.25}}{5} = - 1.5cm{/tex} i.e. object should be held at 1.5 cm in front of objective lens. As v 0 = -5u 0 {tex}\therefore {v_0} = - 5\left( { - 1.5} \right) = 7.5cm{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{1}{{{v_e}}} - \frac{1}{{{u_e}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_e}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{{u_e}}} = \frac{1}{{{v_e}}} - \frac{1}{{{f_e}}} = \frac{1}{{ - 25}} - \frac{1}{5} = - \frac{6}{{25}}{/tex} {tex}{u_e} = - \frac{{25}}{6} = - 4.17cm{/tex} Separation between the objective lens and eyepiece {tex} = \left| {{u_e}} \right| + \left| {{v_0}} \right|{/tex} = 4.17 + 7.5 = 11.67 cm
solution_html:
In normal adjustment, image is formed at least distance of distinct vision,
d = 25 cm
Angular magnification of eyepiece {tex} = \left( {1 + \frac{D}{{{f_e}}}} \right){/tex}
{tex} = \left( {1 + \frac{{25}}{5}} \right) = 6{/tex}
Since the total magnification is 30, magnification of objective lens,
{tex}m = \frac{{30}}{6} = 5{/tex}
Now, {tex} m = - \frac{{{v_0}}}{{{u_0}}} = 5{/tex} or {tex}{v_0} = -5{u_0}{/tex}
As {tex}\frac{1}{{{v_0}}} - \frac{1}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_0}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{{ - 5{u_0}}} - \frac{1}{{{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{1.25}}{/tex}
{tex} - \frac{6}{{5{u_0}}} = \frac{1}{{1.25}}{/tex}
{tex}{u_0} = - \frac{{6 \times 1.25}}{5} = - 1.5cm{/tex}
i.e. object should be held at 1.5 cm in front of objective lens.
As v0 = -5u0
{tex}\therefore {v_0} = - 5\left( { - 1.5} \right) = 7.5cm{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac{1}{{{v_e}}} - \frac{1}{{{u_e}}} = \frac{1}{{{f_e}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{{{u_e}}} = \frac{1}{{{v_e}}} - \frac{1}{{{f_e}}} = \frac{1}{{ - 25}} - \frac{1}{5} = - \frac{6}{{25}}{/tex}
{tex}{u_e} = - \frac{{25}}{6} = - 4.17cm{/tex}
Separation between the objective lens and eyepiece {tex} = \left| {{u_e}} \right| + \left| {{v_0}} \right|{/tex}
= 4.17 + 7.5 = 11.67 cm

question_text: A small telescope has an objective lens of focal length 140 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 5.0 cm. What is the magnifying power of the telescope for viewing distant objects when the telescope is in normal adjustment (i.e. when the final image is at infinity) the final image is formed at the least distance of distinct vision (25 cm)?
question_html:
A small telescope has an objective lens of focal length 140 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 5.0 cm. What is the magnifying power of the telescope for viewing distant objects when

  1. the telescope is in normal adjustment (i.e. when the final image is at infinity)
  2. the final image is formed at the least distance of distinct vision (25 cm)?
solution_text: Given, f 0 = 140, f e = 5 cm Magnifying power {tex} = \frac{{{f_0}}}{{{f_e}}} = - \frac{{140}}{5}{/tex} = -28 [For normal or final image at infinity] Magnifying power {tex} = - \frac{{{f_0}}}{{{f_e}}}\left( {1 + \frac{{{f_e}}}{D}} \right){/tex} [For final image at least distinct of vision] {tex} = - \frac{{140}}{5}\left( {1 + \frac{5}{{25}}} \right) = - 33.6{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, f0 = 140, fe = 5 cm

  1. Magnifying power {tex} = \frac{{{f_0}}}{{{f_e}}} = - \frac{{140}}{5}{/tex} = -28 [For normal or final image at infinity]
  2. Magnifying power {tex} = - \frac{{{f_0}}}{{{f_e}}}\left( {1 + \frac{{{f_e}}}{D}} \right){/tex} [For final image at least distinct of vision]
    {tex} = - \frac{{140}}{5}\left( {1 + \frac{5}{{25}}} \right) = - 33.6{/tex}

question_text: For the telescope described the telescope is in normal adjustment (i.e., when the final image is at infinity)? A small telescope has an objective lens of focal length 140cm and an eyepiece of focal length 5.0cm. What is the separation between the objective lens and the eyepiece? If this telescope is used to view a 100 m tall tower 3 km away, what is the height of the image of the tower formed by the objective lens? What is the height of the final image of the tower if it is formed at 25cm?
question_html:
1. For the telescope described the telescope is in normal adjustment (i.e., when the final image is at infinity)? A small telescope has an objective lens of focal length 140cm and an eyepiece of focal length 5.0cm. What is the separation between the objective lens and the eyepiece?
  2. If this telescope is used to view a 100 m tall tower 3 km away, what is the height of the image of the tower formed by the objective lens?
  3. What is the height of the final image of the tower if it is formed at 25cm?
solution_text: Focal length of objective lens is f 0 = 140 cm Focal length of an eyepiece is f e = 5 cm At normal adjustment, the separation between the objective lens and the eyepiece is f e + f 0 = 145 cm Distance of tower is 3000 m; h 1 = 100m angle subtended by tower at telescope is {tex}\theta _1 = \frac {h_1}{u} = \frac {1}{30} rad{/tex} angle subtended by image, {tex}\theta _2 = \frac {h_2}{f_{0}} = \frac {h_2}{140} rad{/tex} as two angles are equal, {tex}{1\over 30}={ h_2\over 140}{/tex} hence h 2 = 4.7 cm Image formed at d = 25 cm m {tex}=1+\frac {d}{f}=6{/tex} f e is focal length of eyepiece. Height of the image is {tex}m \times h_2 = 6 \times 4.7 = 28.2cm{/tex} Hence, the height of the final image of the tower is 28.2 cm.
solution_html:
1. Focal length of objective lens is f0 = 140 cm
    Focal length of an eyepiece is fe = 5 cm
    At normal adjustment, the separation between the objective lens and the eyepiece is fe + f0 = 145 cm
  2. Distance of tower is 3000 m; h1 = 100m
    angle subtended by tower at telescope is {tex}\theta _1 = \frac {h_1}{u} = \frac {1}{30} rad{/tex}
    angle subtended by image, {tex}\theta _2 = \frac {h_2}{f_{0}} = \frac {h_2}{140} rad{/tex}
    as two angles are equal, {tex}{1\over 30}={ h_2\over 140}{/tex} hence h2 = 4.7 cm
  3. Image formed at d = 25 cm
    m {tex}=1+\frac {d}{f}=6{/tex}
    fe is focal length of eyepiece.
    Height of the image is {tex}m \times h_2 = 6 \times 4.7 = 28.2cm{/tex} Hence, the height of the final image of the tower is 28.2 cm.

question_text: A Cassegrain telescope uses two mirrors as shown in the figure. Such a telescope is built with the mirrors 20 mm apart. If the radius of curvature of large mirror is 220 mm and the small mirror is 140 mm, where will the final image of an object at infinity be?
question_html: A Cassegrain telescope uses two mirrors as shown in the figure. Such a telescope is built with the mirrors 20 mm apart. If the radius of curvature of large mirror is 220 mm and the small mirror is 140 mm, where will the final image of an object at infinity be?
solution_text: Here it is given that radius of curvature of objective mirror, R 1 = 220 mm {tex}\therefore f _ { 1 } = \frac { R _ { 1 } } { 2 } = \frac { 220 } { 2 }{/tex} = 110 mm Radius of curvature of secondary mirror, R 2 = 140 mm {tex}\therefore f _ { 2 } = \frac { R _ { 2 } } { 2 } = \frac { 140 } { 2 }{/tex} = 70 mm Distance between two mirrors, d = 20 mm from objective mirror. Now, for secondary mirror, we have; u = f 1 - d = 110 - 20 = 90 mm Now by using mirror formula we have, {tex}\frac { 1 } { v } + \frac { 1 } { u } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { u }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 70 } - \frac { 1 } { 90 } \Rightarrow v = \frac { 630 } { 2 }{/tex} = 315 mm Hence, the final image will be at 31.5 cm to the right of secondary mirror.
solution_html:
Here it is given that radius of curvature of objective mirror, R1 = 220 mm
{tex}\therefore f _ { 1 } = \frac { R _ { 1 } } { 2 } = \frac { 220 } { 2 }{/tex} = 110 mm
Radius of curvature of secondary mirror, R2 = 140 mm
{tex}\therefore f _ { 2 } = \frac { R _ { 2 } } { 2 } = \frac { 140 } { 2 }{/tex} = 70 mm
Distance between two mirrors, d = 20 mm from objective mirror.
Now, for secondary mirror, we have; u = f1 - d = 110 - 20 = 90 mm
Now by using mirror formula we have,
{tex}\frac { 1 } { v } + \frac { 1 } { u } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 2 } } \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } = \frac { 1 } { f _ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { u }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 70 } - \frac { 1 } { 90 } \Rightarrow v = \frac { 630 } { 2 }{/tex}= 315 mm
Hence, the final image will be at 31.5 cm to the right of secondary mirror.

question_text: Light incident normally on a plane mirror attached to a galvanometer coil retraces backwards as shown in figure. A current in the coil produces a deflection of 3.5° of the mirror. What is the displacement of the reflected spot of light on a screen placed 1.5 m away?
question_html: Light incident normally on a plane mirror attached to a galvanometer coil retraces backwards as shown in figure. A current in the coil produces a deflection of 3.5° of the mirror. What is the displacement of the reflected spot of light on a screen placed 1.5 m away?
solution_text: We know that if the mirror is turned by an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} , the reflected ray turns by an angle 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} . The current in the coil has produced a deflection of 3.5° in the mirror, therefore, the reflected ray will be deflected by an angle 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.5 0 = 7 0 . From the figure, we have {tex}\frac{d}{{1.5}} = \tan {7^0}\left[ {\tan \theta = \frac{{perpendicular}}{{base}}} \right]{/tex} or d = 1.5 tan 7° = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.123 = 0.184 m = 18.4 cm

question_text: The given below figure shows an equiconvex lens (of refractive index 1.50) in contact with a liquid layer on top of a plane mirror. A small needle with its tip on the principal axis is moved along the axis until its inverted image is found at the position of the needle. The distance of the needle from the lens is measured to be 45.0 cm. The liquid is removed and the experiment is repeated. The new distance is measured to be 30.0 cm. What is the refractive index of the liquid?
question_html: The given below figure shows an equiconvex lens (of refractive index 1.50) in contact with a liquid layer on top of a plane mirror. A small needle with its tip on the principal axis is moved along the axis until its inverted image is found at the position of the needle. The distance of the needle from the lens is measured to be 45.0 cm. The liquid is removed and the experiment is repeated. The new distance is measured to be 30.0 cm. What is the refractive index of the liquid?
solution_text: Focal length of the convex lens, f 1 = 30 cm. Focal length of plane concave lens of liquid be = f 2 and, Combined focal length, F = 45.0 cm As, {tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{F}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{F}-\frac{1}{f_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{45}-\frac{1}{30}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{90}{/tex} f 2 = -90 cm For glass lens, ket R 1 = R, R 2 = -R Using lens maker's formula, {tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex} We have, {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{30}=\left(\frac{3}{2}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{R}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{R}=\frac{1}{R}{/tex} Thus, R = 30 cm For liquid lens, R 1 = -R = -30.0 cm {tex}\mathrm{R}_{2}=\infty{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\left(\mu_{l}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\mu_{l}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{-30}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \ \frac{1}{-90}=\left(\mu_{l}-1\right) \times \frac{1}{-30}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \ \left(\mu_{l}-1\right)=\frac{30}{90}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \ \left(\mu_{l}-1\right)=\frac{30}{90}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\mu_{l}=1+\frac{1}{3}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex} ,this is required refractive index
solution_html:
Focal length of the convex lens, f1 = 30 cm.
Focal length of plane concave lens of liquid be = f2
and,
Combined focal length, F = 45.0 cm
As, {tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{F}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\frac{1}{F}-\frac{1}{f_{1}}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{1}{45}-\frac{1}{30}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{1}{90}{/tex}
f2 = -90 cm
For glass lens, ket R1 = R, R2 = -R
Using lens maker's formula,
{tex}\frac{1}{f_{1}}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex}
We have,
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{30}=\left(\frac{3}{2}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{R}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{R}=\frac{1}{R}{/tex}
Thus, R = 30 cm
For liquid lens,
R1 = -R = -30.0 cm
{tex}\mathrm{R}_{2}=\infty{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\left(\mu_{l}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\mu_{l}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{-30}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \ \frac{1}{-90}=\left(\mu_{l}-1\right) \times \frac{1}{-30}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \ \left(\mu_{l}-1\right)=\frac{30}{90}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \ \left(\mu_{l}-1\right)=\frac{30}{90}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\mu_{l}=1+\frac{1}{3}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex} ,this is required refractive index
